question: The 'hard problem of consciousness' (David Chalmers) states
options: Gödel's incompleteness theorems prove human mathematical insight transcends any formal computational system
The computational and physical resources required to produce a system — a complexity measure beyond entropy
Physical explanations of brain processes cannot explain why there is subjective experience at all
The 10⁵⁰⁰ or more possible vacuum states, making it unclear which describes our universe
answer: Physical explanations of brain processes cannot explain why there is subjective experience at all

question: The concept of 'Integrated Information Theory' (IIT) of consciousness proposes
options: Filaments of dark matter and galaxies surrounding vast cosmic voids
Whether intelligence is a thermodynamic inevitability — a universe developing observers that maximise information processing
Gödel's incompleteness theorems prove human mathematical insight transcends any formal computational system
Consciousness corresponds to a system's integrated information (phi) — a quantity measuring causal interdependence
answer: Consciousness corresponds to a system's integrated information (phi) — a quantity measuring causal interdependence

question: The 'free energy principle' (Karl Friston) unifies
options: Multiple training signals or modalities reinforce each other to produce capabilities greater than the sum of parts
Perception, action and learning under a single framework: all adaptive systems minimise variational free energy
Macro-level descriptions can have more causal power than micro-level ones — suggesting higher-level causation is real
When a trained model develops internal optimisers with objectives that diverge from the training objective
answer: Perception, action and learning under a single framework: all adaptive systems minimise variational free energy
